rationale: Park et al. (2000) is used in the theory development section to define additive versus subtractive option framing and the established effect on option uptake, not merely as background. The citing paper then explicitly contrasts CvSS with that paradigm, arguing that CvSS relaxes key constraints (exogenous extreme starting point and unidirectional changes). In the general discussion, the authors again position their contribution as advancing option-framing research and addressing the complexity/regret downside linked to subtractive framing. This indicates a clear conceptual role for the cited paper.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Park et al.'s option-framing results as a benchmark and refines that line of work by proposing CvSS, where starting solutions are consumer-chosen and can be modified in both directions. It positions CvSS as retaining feature-rich choice benefits while mitigating the process-complexity/regret tradeoff associated with subtractive framing.
